macro_summary: Markets enter the week digesting two major concurrent macro shifts: (1) the U.S.-Iran peace MOU signed June 17 at Versailles, reopening the Strait of Hormuz and sending Brent crude down roughly 10% on the week to ~$76/barrel — though analysts warn markets may be pricing in a best-case scenario given minimal actual vessel traffic, unresolved mine clearance, and only a 60-day ceasefire window; and (2) a hawkish pivot from the Fed's June 16-17 FOMC, where Chair Kevin Warsh's debut meeting left rates on hold at 3.50–3.75% but the dot plot showed nine of 18 officials penciling in at least one rate hike by year-end 2026, a stark reversal from March's rate-cut bias. The week ahead is dense with hard catalysts: S&P Global PMI (Tuesday), FedEx Q4 earnings (Tuesday), Micron Q3 earnings (Wednesday), and May PCE + Q1 GDP final revision (Thursday) — making this one of the more consequential data weeks of Q2.
key_events: U.S.-Iran MOU signed June 17 at Versailles; Strait of Hormuz nominally reopened, but vessel traffic remains minimal and a 60-day negotiation window begins
Fed June FOMC (June 16-17): rates held at 3.50–3.75% unanimously; 9 of 18 officials project at least one rate hike in 2026; Warsh drops forward guidance, launches 5 Fed policy task forces
WTI settled ~$76.60 last Thursday, down ~10% on the week; Brent ~$76; analysts see $78–$85 Q4 range if deal holds
Intel +10% Thursday June 18 after Trump announced Apple-Intel U.S. chip manufacturing deal via Truth Social; neither company formally confirmed
Intel 18A-P process entered risk production June 16; Intel Q2 earnings date: July 23, 2026
FedEx Q4 FY2026 earnings: Tuesday June 23 — consensus $5.91 EPS, $24.18B revenue; first post-Freight-spinoff report
Micron Q3 FY2026 earnings: Wednesday June 24 after close — company guidance ~$33.5B rev / $19.15 EPS / ~81% gross margin
May PCE price index + Q1 GDP final revision: Thursday June 26 — key Fed rate-hike input; core PCE was 3.3% YoY in April
Core PCE ticked up to 3.30% YoY in April (from 3.20% in March); headline PCE at 3.80% YoY — well above Fed's 2% target
Nasdaq Composite +2.43% last week, S&P 500 +0.93%; only ~58% of S&P 500 stocks above their 50-day MA — breadth remains narrow

ideas.name: Micron Technology
ideas.thesis: Micron reports fiscal Q3 2026 results on June 24 after close — one of the most consequential semiconductor prints of the year. The company's own guidance calls for ~$33.5B revenue, ~$19.15 non-GAAP EPS, and ~81% gross margin, which would represent over 200% YoY revenue growth. The gross margin figure is particularly significant: at 81% it far exceeds Micron's historical norms and is the cleanest real-time read on whether HBM pricing power is holding. The company has publicly stated its HBM production is sold out through 2026 under binding contracts, giving unusually clear near-term revenue visibility.
ideas.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings report: Wednesday, June 24, 2026 after market close — hard binary event with management guidance on HBM4 production, 2027 supply contracts, and capex trajectory
ideas.edge: Company guidance implies ~$19.15 non-GAAP EPS and ~81% gross margin for Q3; analyst revenue estimates span an unusually wide $33.7B–$40.9B range — the spread itself signals a high-impact event. Options volatility has increased materially into the print. One analyst's $500 price target implies ~40%+ upside from recent levels at 10–12x consensus EPS, a below-market multiple for a company posting 200%+ revenue growth.
ideas.bull_case: HBM sold out at record margins confirms the supercycle is structural rather than cyclical; a guidance raise for Q4 and 2027 supply contract commentary could re-rate the stock meaningfully higher. Nvidia's certification of Micron's HBM4 for the Vera Rubin platform provides a durable anchor to the thesis.
ideas.bear_case: Memory markets have a long history of sharp cycles; SK Hynix holds ~43% HBM market share vs. Micron's ~24%, meaning competitive pressure from a better-resourced rival is real. Any sign of margin compression — even to 75% — would signal loosening supply and could trigger a sharp sell-off in a stock already up ~70% YTD.

ideas.name: FedEx Corporation
ideas.thesis: FedEx reports Q4 FY2026 earnings on June 23 — the first report following the June 1 spinoff of FedEx Freight into an independent publicly traded company. This print is structurally important: investors will get the first clean look at FedEx's post-separation financials and, critically, FY2027 guidance for the leaner, Network 2.0-focused core business. The Freight spinoff removes a lower-margin segment, and management's handling of the retained 19.9% stake in FedEx Freight and the monetization timeline will be a key overhang resolution.
ideas.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings: Tuesday, June 23, 2026 — first post-Freight-spinoff report plus FY2027 guidance; two discrete catalysts in one print (restructuring clarity + forward outlook)
ideas.edge: Wall Street consensus: $5.91 EPS on $24.18B revenue. The stock has been trading near 52-week highs bolstered by Network 2.0 cost savings. The key quantifiable gap: if management can articulate a clear margin expansion roadmap for the stripped-down entity and provide above-consensus FY2027 EPS guidance, the stock trades at a discount to its restructuring value — a concrete setup that either confirms or breaks the bull case on a specific date.
ideas.bull_case: Network 2.0 transformation is ahead of schedule, Freight spinoff eliminates a structural drag, and FY2027 guidance comes in above consensus — triggering a re-rating of FedEx as a leaner, higher-margin logistics pure-play at a time when shipping demand is recovering as Strait of Hormuz trade normalizes.
ideas.bear_case: One-time costs from the Freight separation and MD-11 fleet groundings may depress Q4 GAAP earnings below expectations; if FY2027 guidance disappoints or management is vague on post-spinoff margin targets, the stock's elevated valuation near 52-week highs leaves limited cushion. Global trade volumes remain pressured by residual tariff effects and supply-chain uncertainty from the Iran conflict.

ideas.name: Intel Corporation
ideas.thesis: Intel surged 10%+ on June 18 after Trump announced on Truth Social that Apple agreed to work with Intel to design and build chips in the U.S. — part of a broader pattern of government-backed foundry wins (Nvidia, Tesla's TeraFab). Intel's 18A-P process entered risk production on June 16, providing a technical milestone to back up the narrative. The stock has now risen 464% over the past 12 months and carries a ~$608B market cap. The Apple deal remains formally unconfirmed by either company, making the July 23 Q2 earnings call the next hard catalyst where management can either confirm or clarify the foundry pipeline.
ideas.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings: July 23, 2026 — management will face direct questions about Apple foundry commitment, 18A-P customer pipeline, and whether the deal translates into signed volume contracts; any formal confirmation could re-rate the stock materially
ideas.edge: One Stocktwits analyst note flagged that winning Apple as a foundry customer 'would roughly double Intel's total revenue (~$53B annual run rate currently)' — a concrete revenue-doubling scenario if fully executed. However, the stock has already priced in significant foundry optimism; the edge going forward is the delta between deal confirmation (catalyst in ~4 weeks) and current price which reflects deal speculation, not contract certainty.
ideas.bull_case: Apple formally confirms Intel foundry relationship in coming weeks; CEO Lip-Bu Tan signals multiple additional foundry customers by end of 2026; 18A-P yields prove competitive with TSMC — completing Intel's turnaround story and justifying a sustained premium multiple.
ideas.bear_case: The announcement was a presidential social media post, not a signed contract — neither Intel nor Apple has issued formal confirmation. The stock's 464% 12-month run means expectations are already very elevated; any delay, volume shortfall, or yield disappointment on 18A-P could trigger a sharp correction. The U.S. government's 10% equity stake also introduces non-commercial governance risk.

ideas.name: United Airlines Holdings
ideas.thesis: The U.S.-Iran peace MOU and Strait of Hormuz reopening represent a structural positive for airlines: jet fuel prices had surged 121% YoY by April 2026, and IATA estimated the war would add ~$100B in collective industry costs in 2026. United, Delta, and American each surged 4.5–7% on June 15 in the initial reaction. The ongoing tailwind — WTI now ~$76 vs. war-peak prices well above $100 — means margin recovery is still materializing, but much of the immediate pop is behind us. The more durable case is that airlines have NOT yet reset their hedging books or fares to reflect sustained lower fuel costs, meaning operating leverage to continued oil normalization remains.
ideas.catalyst: Continued normalization of Strait of Hormuz vessel traffic over June–July (physical catalyst with a multi-week delivery window); Q2 earnings in mid-July will be the first report reflecting the full oil relief impact on fuel expense lines
ideas.edge: Jet fuel was ~$152/barrel (IATA 2026 projection) vs. ~$90 in 2025 — a 69% YoY cost headwind that is now unwinding. Delta's own disclosure: a $0.01/gallon move in jet fuel = $40M/year in costs. Analysts see $78–$85 Q4 Brent, implying continued fuel savings not yet captured in Q2 guidance. However, airlines mostly lack near-unhedged books, meaning the pass-through is gradual rather than immediate.
ideas.bull_case: Oil stays subdued through summer peak season, airlines flow fuel savings directly to margins without immediate fare reductions (airfare lags oil by weeks), and Q2 earnings in July deliver significant upside vs. the war-inflated cost structure Wall Street modeled earlier this year.
ideas.bear_case: The 60-day ceasefire is fragile — analysts warn the market is pricing in the best-case scenario and vessel traffic through the strait remains minimal. A deal breakdown would spike oil prices back sharply, reversing the fuel tailwind. Additionally, high fares during the war may have suppressed travel demand, meaning airlines face a volume headwind even as costs recover.
